narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer despite the challenges many face in this field. Your background includes an upper-middle-class family, full-time student status, an undergraduate GPA of 2.80, an LSAT score of 31, and notably low rankings in your school's decile for both first-year and third-year grades—placing you in the bottom decile for those periods, well below typical students who average around the middle of their classes. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours, estimated an 80.5% chance that you would fail the bar exam, leading to the unfortunate rejection of your application. This high failure risk stems primarily from your third-year school decile, which had the strongest influence by pushing the prediction heavily toward failure compared to peers who performed better in later years. Your first-year decile ranking followed closely as the next biggest factor, also contributing significantly to the model's concern about your bar exam odds since it was much lower than average. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA offered some counterbalance, nudging the prediction slightly toward passing, though not enough to offset the top issues. Your LSAT score and full-time status provided minor positive influences too, aligning with what works for most students, while your family income had a small push toward failure risk. Overall, your profile stands out from typical applicants—who boast higher deciles, GPAs around 3.21, and LSATs near 37—making the model view you as higher risk for the bar despite your strengths. We understand this decision may feel disheartening, especially given the barriers Black women often encounter in legal education. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later academic performance through additional coursework or tutoring, and highlighting any unique experiences that demonstrate your resilience. Many have turned similar setbacks into success by addressing these key areas—keep pushing, as your passion for justice is clear, and there are programs eager to support diverse talents like yours.
